---
title: "06 — Within-child variability: do consistency measures cohere, and change with age?"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.5
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# The question

A central LEVANTE aim is to extract information about **variability within
children**, not just their average ability. A child's *consistency* —
moment-to-moment, response-to-response, wave-to-wave — may itself be a stable,
meaningful individual difference (and is theorized to index attentional /
executive control and developmental stability).

This notebook is an exploratory first pass. We extract three naive per-child,
per-task variability indices, then ask:

1. **Do the variability measures hang together within a child?** (Is a child who
   is variable on one task / one index variable on others — a general "trait"?)
2. **Do they change with age?**

The three indices:

| Index | What it captures | Naive estimator |
|---|---|---|
| **RT-IIV** | moment-to-moment inconsistency | SD of log reaction time across (correct) trials |
| **Person-misfit** | "surprising" responses given ability | IRT **infit** (info-weighted squared standardized residuals) |
| **Growth deviation** | atypical developmental change | \|individual 2-wave slope − task mean slope\| |

Following the brief, we start with **naive** estimators and treat their
**reliability** as a first-class result — if a naive index is too noisy, that is
the signal to move to model-based estimation.

```{r setup}
source(here::here("common.R"))
sc <- read_rds(here("data/scores_all_sites.rds"))
sites4 <- c("pilot_mpieva_de_main","pilot_uniandes_co_bogota",
            "pilot_uniandes_co_rural","pilot_western_ca_main")
core <- tibble(
  task_id = c("egma-math","matrix-reasoning","mental-rotation","trog","vocab",
              "same-different-selection","hearts-and-flowers","theory-of-mind","memory-game"),
  short   = c("math","matrix","mrot","trog","vocab","sds","hf","tom","mg"))
adap    <- c("egma-math","matrix-reasoning","mental-rotation","same-different-selection","trog","vocab")
bylang  <- c("vocab","theory-of-mind")               # by-language calibration → join params within language
ip <- load_item_parameters()

# one analysis run per child×task (first obs, newest/adaptive version), with final ability θ̂
runs <- sc |> filter(dataset %in% sites4, task_id %in% core$task_id, !is.na(score)) |>
  filter(!(task_id %in% adap) | adaptive) |>
  group_by(user_id, task_id) |> arrange(age) |> slice(1) |> ungroup() |>
  select(run_id, user_id, task_id, short, theta = score, age, language)  # sc already carries `short`
```

# 1. Extracting the indices (and their reliability)

For each task we load trial data once and compute, per run: RT-IIV (SD of
log-RT on correct trials), and **infit** person-misfit using the published item
parameters (Rasch/2PL; joined within language for the by-language tasks) and the
child's final ability θ̂. Split-half reliabilities (odd/even trials,
Spearman-Brown) tell us whether each naive index is stable enough to interpret.

```{r extract, cache=TRUE}
sb <- function(a, b){ r <- cor(a, b, use = "complete.obs"); 2*r/(1+r) }
extract_task <- function(tid){
  rn <- runs |> filter(task_id == tid)
  tr <- load_levante_trials(task_ids = tid) |>
    filter(run_id %in% rn$run_id, !is.na(correct)) |>
    left_join(rn |> select(run_id, theta, language), by = "run_id")
  # join item parameters (within language for by-language tasks)
  ipx <- ip |> filter(task_id == tid)
  if (tid %in% bylang) {
    tr <- tr |> inner_join(ipx |> transmute(language = subset, item_uid, b = difficulty, a = discrimination),
                           by = c("item_uid","language"))
  } else {
    tr <- tr |> inner_join(ipx |> transmute(item_uid, b = difficulty, a = discrimination) |>
                             distinct(item_uid, .keep_all = TRUE), by = "item_uid")
  }
  tr <- tr |> mutate(P = pmin(pmax(1/(1+exp(-a*(theta-b))), .001), .999),
                     w = P*(1-P), r2 = (correct - P)^2)
  # run-level indices
  iiv <- tr |> filter(!is.na(rt_numeric), rt_numeric > 200, rt_numeric < 30000, correct == 1) |>
    group_by(run_id) |> filter(n() >= 10) |>
    summarise(iiv = sd(log(rt_numeric)), meanlrt = mean(log(rt_numeric)), .groups = "drop")
  infit <- tr |> group_by(run_id) |> filter(n() >= 10) |>
    summarise(infit = sum(r2)/sum(w), .groups = "drop")
  idx <- rn |> select(run_id, user_id, short, theta, age) |>
    left_join(iiv, by = "run_id") |> left_join(infit, by = "run_id")
  # split-half reliability
  sh_iiv <- tr |> filter(!is.na(rt_numeric), rt_numeric > 200, rt_numeric < 30000, correct == 1) |>
    group_by(run_id) |> filter(n() >= 12) |> mutate(h = row_number() %% 2) |>
    group_by(run_id, h) |> summarise(v = sd(log(rt_numeric)), .groups = "drop") |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = h, values_from = v, names_prefix = "h")
  sh_inf <- tr |> group_by(run_id) |> filter(n() >= 12) |> mutate(h = row_number() %% 2) |>
    group_by(run_id, h) |> summarise(v = sum(r2)/sum(w), .groups = "drop") |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = h, values_from = v, names_prefix = "h")
  list(idx = idx,
       rel = tibble(short = core$short[core$task_id == tid],
                    n = nrow(idx),
                    iiv_rel   = if (nrow(sh_iiv) > 3) sb(sh_iiv$h0, sh_iiv$h1) else NA,
                    infit_rel = if (nrow(sh_inf) > 3) sb(sh_inf$h0, sh_inf$h1) else NA))
}
ex <- map(core$task_id, extract_task)
index_runs <- map_dfr(ex, "idx")
rel_tab    <- map_dfr(ex, "rel")
```

```{r rel-table}
rel_tab |> arrange(desc(iiv_rel)) |> mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 2))) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Split-half reliability of the naive indices (NA = no RT, i.e. memory-game)")
```

**Reliability is the headline.** RT-IIV is reliable enough to use on most tasks
(≈ 0.45–0.85, highest where trials are many — H&F, vocab). Person-misfit (infit)
is reliable on some tasks (H&F 0.94, mental rotation 0.92, ToM 0.69, math 0.59)
but **near-zero or negative on others** (TROG, Same & Different) — naive infit is
simply too noisy there. We carry forward only tasks where each index clears a
modest reliability bar (≥ 0.4).

## Two caveats baked in

```{r misfit-caveats}
# infit mean by task (well-calibrated ≈ 1) and its confound with ability
index_runs |> group_by(short) |>
  summarise(infit_mean = mean(infit, na.rm = TRUE),
            `infit vs |θ|` = cor(infit, abs(theta), use = "complete.obs"), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(infit_mean)) |> mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 2))) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Person-misfit: calibration (mean infit ≈ 1 = good) and the ability confound")
```

- **Matrix-reasoning misfit is unusable** (infit mean ≫ 1): its adaptive engine
  administers items far harder than the children's ability (item b ≈ 2.2 vs
  θ ≈ −1.3), so 4-AFC **guessing** dominates the residuals. We exclude matrix
  from the misfit analyses.
- **Misfit is confounded with ability** (infit vs |θ| ≈ 0.1–0.7, as anticipated):
  extreme-ability children look more "surprising" because they see more
  off-target items. We **partial out ability** in all misfit analyses below.

# 2. Do the variability measures hang together?

We keep indices on tasks where they are at least modestly reliable
(reliability ≥ 0.4), z-score each within task, and ask two questions.

```{r keep-reliable}
iiv_ok   <- rel_tab |> filter(iiv_rel   >= 0.4) |> pull(short)
infit_ok <- setdiff(rel_tab |> filter(infit_rel >= 0.4) |> pull(short), "matrix")
zt <- function(x) as.numeric(scale(x))
zi <- index_runs |> group_by(short) |> mutate(iiv_z = zt(iiv), infit_z = zt(infit),
                                              theta_z = zt(theta)) |> ungroup()
```

## (a) Generality across tasks (within an index)

If RT-IIV is a trait, a child variable on one task should be variable on
others. We correlate each index across tasks and summarise the average
off-diagonal correlation (a child-level "g of variability").

```{r generality}
cross_task_r <- function(df, var, tasks){
  w <- df |> filter(short %in% tasks) |> select(user_id, short, val = all_of(var)) |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = short, values_from = val)
  cm <- cor(w |> select(-user_id), use = "pairwise")
  mean(cm[upper.tri(cm)], na.rm = TRUE)
}
tibble(index = c("RT-IIV","Person-misfit (infit)"),
       tasks = c(paste(iiv_ok, collapse=","), paste(infit_ok, collapse=",")),
       mean_cross_task_r = c(cross_task_r(zi, "iiv_z", iiv_ok),
                             cross_task_r(zi, "infit_z", infit_ok))) |>
  mutate(mean_cross_task_r = round(mean_cross_task_r, 3)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Average cross-task correlation of each variability index (within child)")
```

Cross-task generality is **near zero** for both indices (mean r ≈ 0.05–0.09).
A child who is RT-variable on math is essentially *no more likely* to be
RT-variable on vocabulary. Even allowing for attenuation by the indices' modest
reliabilities, this is far too low to support a general "variability trait" —
naive within-task variability is overwhelmingly **task-specific**.

## (b) Do the different indices correlate, beyond ability?

We aggregate each index to the child level (mean of within-task z-scores), add a
crude ability composite (mean within-task z-θ̂) and the naive growth-deviation
index (built next), and correlate — first raw, then partialling ability.

```{r growth, cache=TRUE}
gr <- sc |> filter(dataset %in% sites4, task_id %in% core$task_id, !is.na(score)) |>
  group_by(user_id, short) |> arrange(age) |> mutate(w = row_number()) |>
  filter(w <= 2, max(w) >= 2) |> ungroup()
gw <- gr |> select(user_id, short, w, score, score_se, age) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = w, values_from = c(score, score_se, age)) |>
  mutate(dage = age_2 - age_1, slope = (score_2 - score_1)/dage) |>
  filter(is.finite(slope), dage > 0.1)
# difference-score reliability per task: 1 - error var / observed var
grel <- gw |> group_by(short) |>
  summarise(growth_rel = 1 - mean((score_se_1^2 + score_se_2^2)/dage^2) / var(slope),
            n = n(), .groups = "drop")
gw <- gw |> group_by(short) |>
  mutate(growth_dev = abs(slope - mean(slope)), growth_dev_z = as.numeric(scale(growth_dev))) |> ungroup()
grel |> arrange(desc(growth_rel)) |> mutate(growth_rel = round(growth_rel, 2)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Reliability of the naive 2-wave growth-deviation index")
```

The naive 2-wave growth-deviation index is **moderately reliable for the
fast-growing, well-measured tasks** (H&F 0.77, math 0.59, vocab 0.48, mental
rotation 0.40) but **unreliable for the rest** (memory & Same-&-Different low;
TROG & ToM negative) — wherever true between-child slope variance is small
relative to the θ̂ measurement error carried by a two-point difference. So it is
usable on a few tasks but fragile overall; more waves and error-aware shrinkage
would stabilise it. (But §4.2 shows even this is optimistic: it credits the EAP
posterior SE as the *only* source of error — a proper random-slope model that
lets occasion variance compete finds individual growth differences are not yet
identifiable from two waves.)

```{r child-level}
child <- zi |> group_by(user_id) |>
  summarise(iiv_g   = mean(iiv_z[short %in% iiv_ok], na.rm = TRUE),
            misfit_g = mean(infit_z[short %in% infit_ok], na.rm = TRUE),
            ability  = mean(theta_z, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(gw |> group_by(user_id) |> summarise(growth_g = mean(growth_dev_z, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop"),
            by = "user_id")
M <- child |> select(iiv_g, misfit_g, growth_g, ability)
cmat <- cor(M, use = "pairwise")
# ability-partialled correlations among the 3 variability indices
pr <- function(a, b, c){ d <- na.omit(child[, c(a, b, c)]); resid_a <- resid(lm(d[[a]] ~ d[[c]]))
  resid_b <- resid(lm(d[[b]] ~ d[[c]])); cor(resid_a, resid_b) }
bind_rows(
  tibble(pair = "IIV — misfit",  raw = cmat["iiv_g","misfit_g"],   partial_ability = pr("iiv_g","misfit_g","ability")),
  tibble(pair = "IIV — growth",  raw = cmat["iiv_g","growth_g"],   partial_ability = pr("iiv_g","growth_g","ability")),
  tibble(pair = "misfit — growth", raw = cmat["misfit_g","growth_g"], partial_ability = pr("misfit_g","growth_g","ability"))
) |> mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 3))) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Do the variability indices correlate at the child level? (raw and ability-partialled)")
```

```{r ability-link}
tibble(index = c("RT-IIV","Person-misfit","Growth deviation"),
       r_with_ability = c(cmat["iiv_g","ability"], cmat["misfit_g","ability"], cmat["growth_g","ability"])) |>
  mutate(r_with_ability = round(r_with_ability, 3)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Is 'variability' just (low) ability? Correlation of each index with the ability composite")
```

Two things stand out. First, the three indices are **mutually uncorrelated**
(all |r| < 0.1, raw or ability-partialled) — they do not converge on a common
"variability" dimension. Second, the **ability** picture splits sharply:
RT-IIV and growth-deviation are essentially **unrelated to ability** (r ≈ ±0.05,
so they are *not* just low-ability in disguise), but **person-misfit is heavily
tied to ability** (r ≈ −0.61) — lower-ability children look far more
"surprising," exactly the guessing/confound concern flagged at the outset. Naive
misfit is, to a large degree, an inverse ability measure.

For each (reliable) index we regress on age — pooled across tasks with a task
random effect — and plot the per-task trends.

```{r age-trends, fig.height=4.8}
library(lme4)
age_fit <- function(df, var, tasks){
  d <- df |> filter(short %in% tasks, !is.na(.data[[var]]))
  m <- lmer(reformulate(c("age","(1|short)"), response = var), data = d)
  fixef(m)["age"]
}
slopes <- tibble(
  index = c("RT-IIV","Person-misfit"),
  per_year = c(age_fit(zi |> rename(v = iiv),   "v", iiv_ok),
               age_fit(zi |> rename(v = infit), "v", infit_ok)))
slopes |> mutate(per_year = round(per_year, 3)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Age slope of each index (pooled, task random intercept; z-units & raw infit/IIV)")

bind_rows(
  zi |> filter(short %in% iiv_ok)   |> transmute(short, age, value = iiv,   index = "RT-IIV (SD log RT)"),
  zi |> filter(short %in% infit_ok) |> transmute(short, age, value = infit, index = "Person-misfit (infit)")
) |>
  ggplot(aes(age, value, color = short)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~index, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_color_viridis_d(name = NULL) +
  labs(x = "Age (years)", y = "Index value",
       title = "Age trends differ by index",
       subtitle = "Person-misfit declines with age (but tracks rising ability); moment-to-moment RT variability is roughly flat")
```

Only **person-misfit declines with age** (≈ −0.14/yr); **RT-IIV is essentially
flat** (pooled slope ≈ 0). And the misfit decline is hard to read as maturing
*consistency* per se: because misfit is so strongly tied to ability (above) and
ability rises with age, the age trend in misfit may largely reflect children
making fewer lucky guesses as they master more items — not an independent gain
in response consistency.

# Synthesis — naive (first-order) indices

```{r save}
write_rds(list(rel = rel_tab, growth_rel = grel, child = child, cor = cmat, age_slopes = slopes),
          here::here("data/within_child_variability_06.rds"))
```

A first, honest pass — and the headline is a **null on coherence**:

- **The variability indices do not hang together.** Within an index, cross-task
  generality is ≈ 0 (a child variable on math is no more variable on vocab);
  across indices, the three are mutually uncorrelated (|r| < 0.1). With naive
  estimators there is **no general "variability trait"** — within-task
  variability is overwhelmingly task-specific.
- **RT-IIV is the cleanest index but not (yet) a trait.** It is reliably measured
  on most tasks (≈ 0.45–0.85) and is **not** an ability proxy (r ≈ −0.05) — but
  it is task-specific and **flat with age**. So it is a sound per-task measure
  whose cross-situational meaning is unclear here.
- **Person-misfit is largely an inverse ability measure.** Naive infit is
  reliable on only some tasks, **broken on poorly-targeted adaptive tasks**
  (matrix), and **heavily confounded with ability** (r ≈ −0.61); its age decline
  probably just tracks fewer lucky guesses as ability rises. It needs
  **guessing-aware person fit** before it can be trusted as anything other than
  ability (done in §4.1 — no new 3PL required, the recorded chance level suffices).
- **Growth-deviation is salvageable on some tasks.** Two-wave reliability is
  moderate where growth is fast and well-measured (H&F 0.77, math 0.59, vocab
  0.48) but poor elsewhere — usable in places, fragile overall.

**How to read this:** the null on coherence is partly substantive (these may
genuinely be distinct, task-bound phenomena) and partly methodological — naive
estimators are noisy and, for misfit, badly confounded, so any true common
signal would be heavily attenuated. That is exactly the contingency the brief
anticipated, and it motivates a **model-based second pass**: (1) replace naive
infit with a guessing-aware person-fit statistic from the scoring model;
(2) estimate individual growth as shrunken, error-propagating random slopes
(and revisit as more waves arrive); (3) model RT-IIV with an explicit
person×task variance-components model rather than per-run SDs; then re-ask
whether — disattenuated — the indices cohere into a within-child variability
construct.

# 4. Model-based upgrades

We keep the naive analyses above (we will always want those first-order views)
and add the model-based versions here.

**A note on guessing.** The current scoring models are **Rasch / 2PL — they
carry no guessing (lower-asymptote) parameter** (the item bank stores only
difficulty, plus discrimination for 39 2PL items). But the multiple-choice
**chance** level *is* recorded per item (0.25 for the 4-AFC tasks, 0.5 for
2-AFC, etc.). So we do **not** need to fit a new 3PL: we fold the known chance
into the person-fit response function as a fixed lower asymptote,
P = c + (1 − c)·IRF, using the existing parameters.

## 4.1 Guessing-aware person-fit

```{r upg-misfit, cache=TRUE}
upg_mf <- map_dfr(core$task_id, function(tid){
  rn <- runs |> filter(task_id == tid)
  tr <- load_levante_trials(task_ids = tid) |> filter(run_id %in% rn$run_id, !is.na(correct)) |>
    left_join(rn |> select(run_id, theta, language), by = "run_id")
  ipx <- ip |> filter(task_id == tid)
  if (tid %in% bylang) tr <- tr |> inner_join(ipx |> transmute(language = subset, item_uid, b = difficulty, a = discrimination), by = c("item_uid","language"))
  else tr <- tr |> inner_join(ipx |> transmute(item_uid, b = difficulty, a = discrimination) |> distinct(item_uid, .keep_all = TRUE), by = "item_uid")
  tr <- tr |> mutate(c = ifelse(is.na(chance), 0, chance), irf = 1/(1+exp(-a*(theta-b))),
                     Pn = pmin(pmax(irf, .001), .999), Pg = pmin(pmax(c + (1-c)*irf, .001), .999))
  agg <- tr |> group_by(run_id) |> filter(n() >= 10) |>
    summarise(naive = sum((correct-Pn)^2)/sum(Pn*(1-Pn)),
              guess = sum((correct-Pg)^2)/sum(Pg*(1-Pg)), .groups = "drop") |>
    left_join(rn |> select(run_id, theta), by = "run_id")
  sh <- tr |> group_by(run_id) |> filter(n() >= 12) |> mutate(h = row_number() %% 2) |>
    group_by(run_id, h) |> summarise(v = sum((correct-Pg)^2)/sum(Pg*(1-Pg)), .groups = "drop") |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = h, values_from = v, names_prefix = "h")
  r <- cor(sh$h0, sh$h1, use = "complete.obs")
  tibble(short = core$short[core$task_id == tid],
         `naive infit` = mean(agg$naive), `guess-aware infit` = mean(agg$guess),
         `naive vs θ` = cor(agg$naive, agg$theta, use = "complete.obs"),
         `guess-aware vs θ` = cor(agg$guess, agg$theta, use = "complete.obs"),
         `guess-aware reliability` = 2*r/(1+r))
})
upg_mf |> mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 2))) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Guessing-aware vs naive person-fit: calibration, ability confound, reliability")
```

Folding in the chance level **fixes the calibration** everywhere — every infit
mean lands near 1 (matrix drops from 4.2 to 0.9, now usable) — and **removes
most of the ability confound** (matrix −0.66 → −0.05). But the split-half
**reliability of the de-confounded misfit collapses to ≈ 0** (the split-half
correlations scatter around zero). The implication is decisive: the naive
infit's apparent reliability was *itself* the ability/guessing confound. Once we
score "surprise" properly, there is **almost no stable individual-difference
signal** in person-fit at these item counts. Person-fit is not a usable
variability trait here — not because it is noisy, but because, de-confounded,
there is little real between-child variance to measure.

## 4.2 Model-based growth (shrunken random slopes)

```{r upg-growth, cache=TRUE}
grl <- sc |> filter(dataset %in% sites4, task_id %in% core$task_id, !is.na(score)) |>
  group_by(user_id, short) |> filter(n() >= 2) |> ungroup() |> mutate(age_c = age - 8)
upg_growth <- map_dfr(sort(unique(grl$short)), function(s){
  d <- grl |> filter(short == s)
  m <- tryCatch(suppressMessages(suppressWarnings(
    lme4::lmer(score ~ age_c + (age_c | user_id), data = d,
               control = lme4::lmerControl(check.conv.singular = "ignore")))), error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(m)) return(tibble(short = s, slope_sd = NA, resid_sd = NA, slope_reliability = NA))
  vc <- as.data.frame(lme4::VarCorr(m))
  ss <- vc$sdcor[vc$grp == "user_id" & vc$var1 == "age_c" & is.na(vc$var2)]
  rs <- vc$sdcor[vc$grp == "Residual"]
  tibble(short = s, slope_sd = ss, resid_sd = rs, slope_reliability = ss^2/(ss^2 + rs^2))
})
upg_growth |> arrange(desc(slope_reliability)) |> mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 3))) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Random-slope LMM per task: reliable between-child slope variance is negligible")
```

With only two waves the random-slope models are **near-singular** and recover
**negligible reliable between-child slope variance** (slope reliability ≈ 0–0.06).
This *revises* the optimistic naive 2-wave reliabilities above: those credited
the EAP posterior SE as the only error term, but a model that lets
occasion-to-occasion variance compete finds individual growth differences are
**not yet identifiable** from two points. Growth-based variability needs **≥ 3
waves** before it can be estimated per child.

## 4.3 RT-IIV as a variance-components model

Rather than a per-run SD, we model log RT-variability across all
person×task runs and ask what share is a **stable cross-task person property**.

```{r upg-iiv, cache=TRUE}
iiv_run <- map_dfr(setdiff(core$task_id, "memory-game"), function(tid){
  rn <- runs |> filter(task_id == tid)
  load_levante_trials(task_ids = tid) |>
    filter(run_id %in% rn$run_id, !is.na(rt_numeric), rt_numeric > 200, rt_numeric < 30000, correct == 1) |>
    group_by(run_id, user_id, short) |> filter(n() >= 10) |>
    summarise(logvar = log(var(log(rt_numeric))), .groups = "drop")
})
vcm <- suppressMessages(lme4::lmer(logvar ~ 1 + (1 | user_id) + (1 | short), data = iiv_run))
vc <- as.data.frame(lme4::VarCorr(vcm)); tot <- sum(vc$vcov)
tibble(component = c("person (stable trait)","task","residual (person×task + noise)"),
       variance_share = c(vc$vcov[vc$grp=="user_id"], vc$vcov[vc$grp=="short"], vc$vcov[vc$grp=="Residual"])/tot) |>
  mutate(variance_share = round(variance_share, 3)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = glue::glue("Variance decomposition of RT-variability ({nrow(iiv_run)} runs, {n_distinct(iiv_run$user_id)} children)"))
```

Only **~4% of RT-variability is a stable cross-task person property**; the bulk
is task differences (~58%) and within-person/residual (~38%, which still
includes measurement noise, so 4% is if anything an upper-ish bound on the
trait share). The near-zero cross-task correlation in the naive analysis was
therefore **not merely attenuation** — RT variability genuinely is
task-specific, not a general trait, at these ages.

```{r upg-save}
write_rds(list(misfit = upg_mf, growth = upg_growth,
               iiv_var = as.data.frame(lme4::VarCorr(vcm))),
          here::here("data/within_child_variability_06_upgrades.rds"))
```

## Synthesis — model-based upgrades

The model-based pass sharpens, and in one place corrects, the naive picture —
and the verdict is a **robust null** on a general within-child variability trait:

- **Guessing-aware person-fit** (recorded chance as a fixed asymptote, no new
  3PL): fixes calibration and the matrix breakdown and strips out the ability
  confound — but reveals the reliable misfit signal *was* that confound.
  De-confounded person-fit has ≈ 0 reliability; it is not a usable trait here.
- **Model-based growth**: two waves cannot identify individual growth
  differences (slope reliability ≈ 0; models near-singular). The naive
  reliabilities were optimistic. Revisit at ≥ 3 waves.
- **RT-IIV variance components**: a person×task model puts the stable-person
  share at ~4% — overwhelmingly task-specific.

**Implication for LEVANTE.** Measuring within-child variability *as a coherent
individual-difference construct* will need richer designs, not better summaries
of the current single-session scores: more longitudinal waves for growth, and
within-task manipulations purpose-built to elicit and reliably capture
variability (e.g., conditions that provoke lapses, speed–accuracy trade-offs).
RT-IIV remains a clean, reliable **per-task** measure — it is simply not, on
this evidence, a cross-task trait.
